summary: Market sentiment is cautiously optimistic, with European shares extending their rally and global investors adopting a risk-on posture as a potential US-Iran interim peace deal takes shape, though critical details remain unresolved. The primary macro catalyst is the emerging US-Iran agreement, which has already driven oil prices down 5% to three-month lows on expectations that the Strait of Hormuz will reopen and Iranian crude exports will resume immediately upon signing, while a reported $300 billion deal fund signals significant economic scope. At the sector level, energy names face earnings pressure from falling oil prices despite Equinor briefly benefiting from the conflict, AI and tech remain in focus with Databricks posting 80%-plus revenue growth amid margin compression from AI agent costs, Google advancing Android 17, and SpaceX attracting speculative retail frenzy with traders betting on outsized overnight gains. Key risks include deal fragility — Hezbollah warning Iran against signing if Israel remains in Lebanon, Congress being kept in the dark on deal terms, and BMW flagging a China-plus-Iran-war profit squeeze — while Rivian layoffs and Wells Fargo's underwhelming post-asset-cap performance highlight persistent micro-level vulnerabilities. In the near term, markets are likely to remain range-bound but upward-leaning on energy disinflation tailwinds, with volatility hinging on whether Iran formally signs the agreement and Congress's reaction once deal specifics are disclosed.
